title: Terraform State, Locking, and Concurrent Applies
description:
## Two Engineers, One State File

Your team uses Terraform with an S3 remote backend:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "acme-tfstate"
    key            = "prod/network/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "eu-west-1"
    dynamodb_table = "acme-tf-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

Two engineers kick off `terraform apply` against this same state at roughly the same time. A junior asks why the `dynamodb_table` line matters and whether it could be safely removed to simplify the config.

Which statement is correct?

options.text: The DynamoDB table provides a distributed lock: the first apply acquires it and the second is blocked (or errors) until the lock is released, preventing two concurrent writes from corrupting the state file.
DynamoDB stores a backup copy of the state, so it can be removed because S3 versioning already protects against corruption from concurrent applies.
S3 strong read-after-write consistency already serializes the two applies, so the DynamoDB lock is purely advisory and redundant in modern AWS.
The lock only protects `terraform plan`; concurrent `apply` operations are safe because each apply writes to a separate per-engineer state key.

explanation: Terraform writes the entire state file as a unit; two concurrent applies can interleave reads and writes and clobber each other, leaving the recorded state out of sync with real infrastructure. The `dynamodb_table` configures the S3 backend's locking mechanism: before mutating state, Terraform writes a lock item to DynamoDB (conditional put on a unique LockID). The second apply cannot acquire the lock and fails fast with a lock error rather than racing. (b) is wrong: DynamoDB holds the lock metadata, not a state backup; S3 versioning lets you recover an old version but does not prevent the concurrent-write corruption in the first place. (c) is wrong: S3 consistency guarantees ordering of individual object operations, not mutual exclusion across a multi-step read-modify-write apply. (d) is wrong: the lock guards any state-mutating operation (including apply), and both engineers here use the same `key`, so there are no separate per-engineer states. Note also that state can contain sensitive values (passwords, keys) in plaintext, which is why `encrypt = true` matters; and pre-existing resources are brought under management with `terraform import`, not by editing state by hand.

hints.title: What does the lock actually prevent?
hints.body: Think about what happens when two processes each read the whole state, make changes, and write the whole state back. The lock makes that read-modify-write atomic across processes.

hints.title: Backup vs lock
hints.body: S3 versioning is your recovery net. DynamoDB here is the mutual-exclusion mechanism. They solve different problems.
